Educational Codeforces Round 188 Editorial
By awoo, history, 10 days ago, translation,

2204A - Passing the Ball

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

2204A - Passing the Ball

It's possible to solve this problem by carefully implementing the process described in the statement: you should code a for-loop to iterate over all iterations, maintain which student has the ball now, and mark all students which have received the ball at least once in some array.

However, there is a much easier (in terms of code) solution. Consider the first character L in the string ss; let its index be ii. When the student (i−1)(i−1) passes the ball to the student ii, the student ii will pasa it back, then the student (i−1)(i−1) passes the ball back to the student ii, and so on, until the ball has been passed nn times. So, no student after the ii-th one will ever receive the ball, and every student before the ii-th one (including him) will receive the ball at least once.

So, the answer depends only on the first occurrence of L in the string.

Solution (BledDest)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
int main()
{
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
    {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        string s;
        cin >> s;             
        cout << s.find("RL") + 2 << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}

2204B - Right Maximum

Idea: fcspartakm

Tutorial

2204B - Right Maximum

There are many ways to solve this problem. One approach is to divide the elements into two groups: those that will be selected during the operations (group 11) and those that will not be selected (group 22). The number of operations will then be equal to the size of group 11.

Suppose there is an element strictly greater than some element aiai to the left of it. It can be shown that in this case, ii will never be selected in the operations: if the element aiai still exists, then the element to the left and greater than it also exists, which means that the element aiai cannot be the maximum. Thus, every element aiai for which there exists an element to the left that is greater belongs to group 22.

On the other hand, if some element aiai is greater than or equal to all elements to its left, it cannot be removed without being selected: in each operation, all removed elements, except for the selected one, are strictly less than the selected one, which means that for such an element, there is no way to remove it without selecting it. Therefore, all such elements belong to group 11.

Thus, it is sufficient to check for each element whether there exists a greater element to its left. To do this, you can traverse the array from left to right, keeping track of the maximum among all previously encountered elements. If an element is less than the maximum of all previous ones, it belongs to group 22; otherwise, it belongs to group 11.

Solution (BledDest)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;

void solve()
{
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i];
    int mx = 0;
    int ans = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        if(a[i] >= mx) ans++;
        mx = max(mx, a[i]);
    }
    cout << ans << "\n";
}
 
int main()
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
        solve();
    return 0;
}

2204C - Spring

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

2204C - Spring

Let's write a function get(x,y,z,n)get(x,y,z,n) that calculates how many liters of water a person will collect over nn days if they come every xx days, while two other people come every yy and zz days. The answers to the problem will be get(a,b,c,m)get(a,b,c,m), get(b,a,c,m)get(b,a,c,m), and get(c,a,b,m)get(c,a,b,m).

How do we calculate get(x,y,z,n)get(x,y,z,n)? Let's try to divide the days into three categories: those when one person comes, those when two people come, and those when all three come (we will assume that among the people who come, there is always the person who comes every xx days).

How do we count the number of days when only the first person comes? At first glance, it seems that there are ⌊nx⌋⌊nx⌋ of them. However, we will count the days when they come with someone else too; therefore, we need to subtract the number of days when the first and second person come, as well as the number of days when the first and third person come. But we have subtracted the days when all three come too many times, so we need to add that back.

The number of days when two people (including the first) come can be counted similarly: we take all the days when the first and second come, add all the days when the second and third come, and subtract twice the days when all three come (we counted these days twice, but we didn't need to count them at all).

Now we need to understand how to count the number of days when the first and second must come (and possibly the third). These are all days ii for which imodx=0imodx=0 and imody=0imody=0; in other words, imodLCM(x,y)=0imodLCM(x,y)=0, where LCM(x,y)LCM(x,y) is the least common multiple of xx and yy. Thus, there are exactly ⌊nLCM(x,y)⌋⌊nLCM(x,y)⌋ such days. The number of days when the first and third must come (and possibly the second) is counted similarly.

Finally, we need to count the number of days when all three come. The number of such a day must be divisible by both xx, yy, and zz; that is, it must be divisible by LCM(x,y,z)LCM(x,y,z), and there will be ⌊nLCM(x,y,z)⌋⌊nLCM(x,y,z)⌋ such days.

This is one possible implementation. It can be generalized to a larger number of people using the inclusion-exclusion principle, but that was not required in this problem.

Solution (BledDest)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
long long getOne(long long a, long long m)
{
    return m / a;
}
 
long long getTwo(long long a, long long b, long long m)
{
    return m / lcm(a, b);    
}
 
long long getThree(long long a, long long b, long long c, long long m)
{
    return m / lcm(lcm(a, b), c);
}
 
long long get(long long a, long long b, long long c, long long m)
{
    long long c1 = getOne(a, m);
    long long c2 = getTwo(a, b, m) + getTwo(a, c, m);
    long long c3 = getThree(a, b, c, m);
    return (c1 - c2 + c3) * 6 + (c2 - 2 * c3) * 3 + c3 * 2;
}
 
void solve()
{
    long long a, b, c, m;
    cin >> a >> b >> c >> m;                                                                      
    cout << get(a, b, c, m) << " " << get(b, a, c, m) << " " << get(c, a, b, m) << endl;
}
 
int main()
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
        solve();
    return 0;
}

2204D - Alternating Path

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

2204D - Alternating Path

Note that the direction of edges in one connected component does not affect other components. Therefore, the answer to the problem is the sum of the answers for all connected components.

It is difficult to analyze the beauty of a vertex vv by considering infinitely many paths. Let's try to simplify the condition. Let's look at paths of length 22. They indicate that all edges incident to vertex vv must be directed away from it towards its neighbors. Now consider paths of length 33: all edges incident to the neighbors of vv must be directed towards them. And so on.

This means that the vertices can be divided into two classes based on the parity of the distance from vv to them. For even vertices, the incident edges are directed away from them, while for odd vertices, they are directed towards them. Thus, it makes sense that each vertex in a connected component must always have the same parity regardless of the choice of path. If there exist two paths such that the vertex is at an even position in one of them and at an odd position in the other one, then the incident edges must be directed both away from and towards it at the same time, which is impossible.

In other words, a connected component must not contain odd length cycles. This means that the component must be bipartite. If a connected component is not bipartite, then it will always have 00 beautiful vertices.

Let's fix some beatiful vertex vv in a bipartite component. Now all edges are uniquely directed. Moreover, it turns out that all vertices in the same part as vv are also beautiful. Therefore, in the bipartite component, we can choose a part which size will be added to the answer. Obviously, we should take the larger of the two parts.

Overall complexity: O(n+m)O(n+m).

Solution (awoo)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
#define forn(i, n) for (int i = 0; i < int(n); i++)
 
using namespace std;
 
 
int main() {
	cin.tie(0);
	ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
	int t;
	cin >> t;
	while (t--){
		int n, m;
		cin >> n >> m;
		vector<vector<int>> g(n);
		forn(i, m){
			int v, u;
			cin >> v >> u;
			--v, --u;
			g[v].push_back(u);
			g[u].push_back(v);
		}
		vector<int> clr(n, -1);
		int ans = 0;
		forn(i, n) if (clr[i] == -1){
			queue<int> q;
			q.push(i);
			clr[i] = 0;
			vector<int> cnt(2);
			bool ok = true;
			while (!q.empty()){
				int v = q.front();
				q.pop();
				++cnt[clr[v]];
				for (int u : g[v]){
					if (clr[u] == clr[v])
						ok = false;
					else if (clr[u] == -1){
						clr[u] = clr[v] ^ 1;
						q.push(u);
					}
				}
			}
			if (ok) ans += max(cnt[0], cnt[1]);
		}
		cout << ans << '\n';
	}
	return 0;
}

2204E - Sum of Digits (and Again)

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

2204E - Sum of Digits (and Again)

We could iterate through the number from which the string was constructed before shuffling, but that would take too long. Instead, let's iterate through the sum of the digits of that number. It will be no greater than 9⋅|s|9⋅|s|.

Knowing the sum of the digits of the number, we can construct a fairly short "suffix" of the string without the original number itself. For example, if the sum of the digits of the number is 389389, we can understand that the string ends with 389202. If the count of occurrences of each digit in this suffix does not exceed that in the original string (for the original string, we can pre-calculate the count of occurrences of all digits), then we can almost use this suffix. Almost, because we still need to check that the remaining digits sum up to exactly the sum we considered — so that the remaining digits can form a number that gives the desired sum of digits.

If both conditions are met — there are enough digits in the string to form the suffix, and the sum of the remaining digits corresponds to the required one — then the remaining digits can be arranged in non-increasing order (to avoid leading zeros) and prepended to the constructed suffix.

We can check each sum of digits in O(d+m)O(d+m) time, where dd is the base of the numeral system (up to 1010), and mm is the length of the constructed suffix (which does not exceed 1111 characters). After that, as soon as we find a suitable sum, we can construct the answer in O(|s|)O(|s|) time.

Thus, we obtain a solution in O(d2⋅|s|+d⋅m⋅|s|)O(d2⋅|s|+d⋅m⋅|s|).

Solution (BledDest)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
int f(int x)
{
    int res = 0;
    while(x != 0)
    {
        res += x % 10;
        x /= 10;
    }
    return res;
}
 
void solve()
{
    string s;
    cin >> s;
    int n = s.size();
    if(n == 1)
    {
        cout << s << "\n";
        return;
    }
    vector<int> cnt(10);
    int sum_digits = 0;
    for(auto x : s) 
    {
        cnt[x - '0']++;
        sum_digits += x - '0';
    }
    for(int x = 1; x <= n * 9; x++)
    {
        int nx = x;
        string sx = to_string(nx);
        while(nx > 9)
        {
            nx = f(nx);
            sx += to_string(nx);    
        }
        //cout << x << " " << sx << endl;               
        vector<int> need_cnt(10);
        int sum_digits_x = 0;
        for(auto y : sx)
        {
            need_cnt[y - '0']++;
            sum_digits_x += y - '0';
        }
        bool can = true;
        for(int i = 0; i <= 9; i++)
            if(need_cnt[i] > cnt[i]) can = false;
        if(sum_digits - sum_digits_x != x)
            can = false;
        if(can)
        {
            string res = "";
            for(int i = 9; i >= 0; i--)
            {
                for(int j = 0; j < cnt[i] - need_cnt[i]; j++)
                    res.push_back(char('0' + i));
            }
            res += sx;
            cout << res << endl;
            return;
        }
    }
}
 
int main()
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
        solve();
    return 0;
}

2204F - Sum of Fractions

Idea: adedalic

Tutorial

2204F - Sum of Fractions

To begin with, let's understand how to obtain the maximum sum MSF(b,k)MSF(b,k). It can be proven (all proofs are at the end) that it is always optimal to choose the minimum bibi and only increase it. Next, it can be shown that if kk is sufficient to reduce the denominator to 11, it is advantageous to first reduce the denominator to 11, and then increase the numerator. If kk is not sufficient, it is better not to touch the denominator and instead "pour" everything into the numerator.

Thus, we can derive the following formula for MSFMSF. Let bmbm be the minimum, then

  • if k<bmk<bm, then MSF(b,k)=k+1bm+∑i≠m1biMSF(b,k)=k+1bm+∑i≠m1bi;
  • otherwise (k≥bmk≥bm) MSF(b,k)=k+(2−bm)+∑i≠m1biMSF(b,k)=k+(2−bm)+∑i≠m1bi.

Next, since we need to calculate the sum of MSFMSF over all subarrays aa, let's understand what contribution each aiai makes to the total sum. Suppose that for each subarray a[l…r]a[l…r], we choose its leftmost minimum aiai. Then, from the perspective of aiai, all elements to the left of it are strictly greater than it, and all elements to the right are greater than or equal to it. That is, for each element aiai, we are interested in

  • the rightmost position l′l′ such that l′<il′<i and al′≤aial′≤ai;
  • the leftmost position r′r′ such that r′>ir′>i and ar′<aiar′<ai.
In this case, in any segment a[l…r]a[l…r] such that l′<l≤il′<l≤i and i≤r<r′i≤r<r′, the element aiai will be the leftmost minimum. In all other segments, it will not be.

As a result, for each aiai, there are exactly ci=(i−l′)(r′−i)ci=(i−l′)(r′−i) segments in which it is the minimum, and ri=(i+1)(n−i)−ciri=(i+1)(n−i)−ci (0-indexing) segments where it is not the minimum. If aiai is not the minimum, it simply adds ri⋅1airi⋅1ai to all kiki.

If aiai is the minimum, then

  • to all kj<aikj<ai, it adds ci⋅kj+1ai=ciaikj+ciaici⋅kj+1ai=ciaikj+ciai;
  • to all kj≥aikj≥ai, it adds ci⋅(kj+2−ai)=cikj+ci(2−ai)ci⋅(kj+2−ai)=cikj+ci(2−ai).
In both cases, we are just adding a linear function to kjkj, which can be done easily with two prefix sums: it is sufficient to separately store the sum of coefficients in front of kk and the sum of the free terms.

Thus, for each aiai, we find the first position pp such that kp≥aikp≥ai; for all kjkj in the interval [0,p)[0,p), we add ciaikj+ciaiciaikj+ciai; for all kjkj in the interval [p,m)[p,m), we add cikj+ci(2−ai)cikj+ci(2−ai).

In the end, the total complexity is O(n(logM+logm)+m)O(n(log⁡M+log⁡m)+m), where logMlog⁡M comes from taking the inverse for aiai and logmlog⁡m comes from lower_boundlower_bound.

Proofs:

First, let's define how to increase a fraction most profitably. Suppose we want to increase the fraction 1a1a by kk. In general, we get f(x)=1+k−xa−xf(x)=1+k−xa−x, where xx is an integer and 0≤x≤min(k,a−1)0≤x≤min(k,a−1). It is easy to calculate the derivative f′(x)=1+k−a(a−x)2f′(x)=1+k−a(a−x)2 and understand that for k<ak<a, on the interval [0,k][0,k], the value of f′(x)≤0f′(x)≤0, which means the maximum is at x=0x=0. Conversely, for k≥ak≥a, on [0,a−1][0,a−1], the value of f′(x)>0f′(x)>0 and the maximum is at x=a−1x=a−1. Thus, we have proven that for k<ak<a, the maximum fraction is of the form 1+ka1+ka, and for k≥ak≥a, it is 1+k−a+11=k+2−a1+k−a+11=k+2−a.

Secondly, we will prove that it is always beneficial to choose the fraction with the minimum bmbm and only increase it. To do this, let's consider two cases. Case 1: after a total increase of kk, all fractions are less than 11.

By contradiction: suppose that besides the mm-th fraction, we also increased some other fraction ii: the mm-th fraction by x≥0x≥0, and the ii-th fraction by y>0y>0. Notice that it is always better to increase mm by x+1x+1 and ii by y−1y−1:

1+x+1bm+1+y−1bi=1+xbm+1+ybi+1bm−1bi≥1+xbm+1+ybi.1+x+1bm+1+y−1bi=1+xbm+1+ybi+1bm−1bi≥1+xbm+1+ybi.

Case 2: There is at least one fraction that is not less than 11. First, if the mm-th fraction is less than 11 (i.e., there is another fraction ii that is not less than 11), it is beneficial to "swap their increases" xx and yy (thereby increasing the mm-th fraction to one), since

1+xbm+(y+2−bi)≥y+2−bm+1+xbi⇔1+xbm−1+xbi≤1+xbm≤1≤bi−bm.1+xbm+(y+2−bi)≥y+2−bm+1+xbi⇔1+xbm−1+xbi≤1+xbm≤1≤bi−bm.

Secondly, when there is one fraction that is not less than 11, it is beneficial to continue increasing that fraction. Specifically, if we take one unit of increase from any other fraction — its value will decrease by no more than 11, while increasing a fraction that has already reached one will increase it strictly by 11, meaning the total sum will not decrease.

Solution (adedalic)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
#define fore(i, l, r) for(int i = int(l); i < int(r); i++)
#define sz(a) int((a).size())
 
#define x first
#define y second
 
typedef long long li;
typedef long double ld;
typedef pair<int, int> pt;
 
template<class A, class B> ostream& operator <<(ostream& out, const pair<A, B> &p) {
	return out << "(" << p.x << ", " << p.y << ")";
}
template<class A> ostream& operator <<(ostream& out, const vector<A> &v) {
	fore(i, 0, sz(v)) {
		if(i) out << " ";
		out << v[i];
	}
	return out;
}
 
const int INF = int(1e9);
const li INF64 = li(1e18);
const ld EPS = 1e-9;
 
const int MOD = 998'244'353;
 
int add(int a, int b) {
	a += b;
	while (a >= MOD)
		a -= MOD;
	while (a < 0)
		a += MOD;
	return a;
}
int mul(int a, int b) {
	return a * 1ll * b % MOD;
}
int binPow(int a, int k) {
	int ans = 1;
	while (k > 0) {
		if (k & 1)
			ans = mul(ans, a);
		a = mul(a, a);
		k >>= 1;
	}
	return ans;
}
 
int n, m;
vector<int> a, k;
 
inline bool read() {
	if(!(cin >> n >> m))
		return false;
	a.resize(n);
	fore (i, 0, n)
		cin >> a[i];
	k.resize(m);
	fore (i, 0, m)
		cin >> k[i];
	return true;
}
 
inline void solve() {
	vector<int> lf(n, -1), rg(n, n);
	vector<int> st;
	fore (i, 0, n) {
		while (!st.empty() && a[st.back()] > a[i])
			st.pop_back();
		if (!st.empty())
			lf[i] = st.back();
		st.push_back(i);
	}
	st.clear();
	for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
		while (!st.empty() && a[st.back()] >= a[i])
			st.pop_back();
		if (!st.empty())
			rg[i] = st.back();
		st.push_back(i);
	}
 
	int commonSum = 0;
	vector<int> la(m + 1), lk(m + 1), ra(m + 1), rk(m + 1);
 
	//[l, r)
	auto addSeg = [](vector<int> &pS, int l, int r, int val) {
		pS[l] = add(pS[l], val);
		pS[r] = add(pS[r], -val);
	};
 
	fore (i, 0, n) {
		int cnt = mul(i - lf[i], rg[i] - i);
		int rem = add(mul(i + 1, n - i), -cnt);
		int ia = binPow(a[i], MOD - 2);
		commonSum = add(commonSum, mul(rem, ia));
 
		int p = lower_bound(k.begin(), k.end(), a[i]) - k.begin();
		addSeg(la, 0, p, mul(cnt, ia));
		addSeg(lk, 0, p, mul(cnt, ia));
		addSeg(ra, p, m, cnt);
		addSeg(rk, p, m, mul(cnt, add(2, -a[i])));
	}
	fore (i, 0, m) {
		la[i + 1] = add(la[i + 1], la[i]);
		lk[i + 1] = add(lk[i + 1], lk[i]);
		ra[i + 1] = add(ra[i + 1], ra[i]);
		rk[i + 1] = add(rk[i + 1], rk[i]);
	}
 
	fore (i, 0, m) {
		int ans = commonSum;
		ans = add(ans, mul(add(la[i], ra[i]), k[i]));
		ans = add(ans, add(lk[i], rk[i]));
		cout << ans << '\n';
	}
}
 
int main() {
#ifdef _DEBUG
	freopen("input.txt", "r", stdin);
	int tt = clock();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0), cout.tie(0);
	cout << fixed << setprecision(15);
	
	if(read()) {
		solve();
		
#ifdef _DEBUG
		cerr << "TIME = " << clock() - tt << endl;
		tt = clock();
#endif
	}
	return 0;
}

2204G - Grid Path

Idea: Roms

Tutorial

2204G - Grid Path

Since we can only move vertically downwards and not upwards, in each visited row, the traversed cells will form a segment. Additionally, for each pair of adjacent rows, these segments must intersect in at least one column to allow movement from one row to another.

This allows us to write the following dynamic programming: dpi,l,rdpi,l,r is the number of ways to choose segments for the first ii rows if the segment [l,r][l,r] is chosen in the last row. Unfortunately, there are too many states.

We can reduce the number of states as follows: instead of using the segment as the state, we will use the column in which we transition to the next row as the state. However, this may lead to counting the same configuration multiple times if two segments in adjacent rows intersect in more than one column. Therefore, we will consider that between each pair of adjacent rows, we transition in the leftmost column where the corresponding segments intersect; that is, if the segments in adjacent rows are [l1,r1][l1,r1] and [l2,r2][l2,r2], we can only transition between them in column j=min(l1,l2)j=min(l1,l2).

This allows us to write the following dynamic programming relation: dp′i,j,fdpi,j,f′ is the number of ways to fill ii rows if we want to transition to the next row in column jj, and ff is a flag that indicates whether jj is the leftmost visited cell in the ii-th row.

Now, for each row, there are only 2m2m states. This allows us to speed up the dynamic programming using binary matrix exponentiation: we will form the vector [dpi,1,0,dpi,1,1,dpi,2,0,dpi,2,1,…,dpi,m,0,dpi,m,1,si][dpi,1,0,dpi,1,1,dpi,2,0,dpi,2,1,…,dpi,m,0,dpi,m,1,si], where sisi is the sum of the dynamic programming values for the first ii rows; then we will construct a matrix of size (2m+1)×(2m+1)(2m+1)×(2m+1) that transforms this vector into the same vector for i+1i+1. This matrix can be constructed "naively" in O(m4)O(m4). Then we can exponentiate this matrix in O(m3logn)O(m3log⁡n) and compute the dynamic programming values significantly faster.

There is only one problem: the modulus in the problem is not fixed. This is actually to prevent solutions via Berlekamp-Massey, but one of the side effects is that the compiler cannot optimize operations modulo a non-constant modulus, and matrix multiplication works very slowly.

We can speed it up as follows: let's take the modulus as infrequently as possible during matrix multiplication. Let k=2m+1k=2m+1, and we want to multiply two matrices AA and BB of size k×kk×k and write the result in matrix CC. Then Ci,j=∑x=1kAi,x⋅Bx,jCi,j=∑x=1kAi,x⋅Bx,j, and this sum needs to be computed modulo. We can store this sum in an unsigned long long and take it modulo pp every 1616 operations (thanks to the fact that the product Ai,xAi,x and Bx,jBx,j does not exceed 10181018).

There are other optimizations for matrix multiplication, but this one is sufficient. There are also options to create a matrix of size (m+1)×(m+1)(m+1)×(m+1), which would also speed up the solution by 88 times, but that was not required.

Solution (Neon)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
#define forn(i, n) for (int i = 0; i < int(n); ++i)
 
const int K = 301;
 
using mat = vector<vector<int>>;
 
int n, m, mod, k;
 
int add(int x, int y) {
  x += y;
  if (x >= mod) x -= mod;
  return x;
}
 
int mul(int x, int y) {
  return x * 1LL * y % mod;
}
 
unsigned long long aux[K][K];
 
mat mul(mat a, mat b) {
  mat b2(k, vector<int>(k));
  forn(x, k) forn(y, k) b2[x][y] = b[y][x];
  mat c(k, vector<int>(k));
  forn(x, k) forn(y, k) {
    aux[x][y] = 0;
    forn(z, k) {
      aux[x][y] += a[x][z] * 1ll * b2[y][z];
      if((z & 15) == 15)
        aux[x][y] %= mod;
    }
    c[x][y] = aux[x][y] % mod;
  }
  return c;
}
 
mat binpow(mat a, int b) {
  mat c(k, vector<int>(k));
  forn(i, k) c[i][i] = 1;
  while (b) {
    if (b & 1) c = mul(c, a);
    a = mul(a, a);
    b >>= 1;
  }
  return c;
}
 
int main() {
  cin >> n >> m >> mod;
  k = 2 * m + 1;
  mat a(k, vector<int>(k));
  a[k - 1][k - 1] = 1;
  forn(i, m) forn(f, 2) {
    for (int l = f ? 0 : i; l <= i; ++l) {
      for (int r = i; r < m; ++r) {
        a[i * 2 + f][k - 1] = add(a[i * 2 + f][k - 1], 1);
        for (int j = l; j <= r; ++j) {
          a[i * 2 + f][j * 2 + (j == l)] = add(a[i * 2 + f][j * 2 + (j == l)], 1);
        }
      }
    }
  }
  auto res = binpow(a, n);
  cout << res[1][k - 1] << endl;
}
Tutorial of Educational Codeforces Round 188 (Rated for Div. 2)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define int long long

const int MOD = 998244353;

int power(int a, int b) {
    int res = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) res = res * a % MOD;
        a = a * a % MOD;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}

int inv(int x) {
    return power(x, MOD - 2);
}

int32_t main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);

    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<int> a(n), k(m);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i];
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) cin >> k[i];

    // Base: sum of sums of all subarrays
    int c = 0, base = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        c = (c + (i + 1) % MOD * inv(a[i]) % MOD) % MOD;
        base = (base + c) % MOD;
    }

    // Monotonic stack to find contribution ranges
    vector<int> L(n), R(n);
    stack<int> st;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        while (!st.empty() && a[st.top()] > a[i]) st.pop();
        L[i] = st.empty() ? -1 : st.top();
        st.push(i);
    }

    while (!st.empty()) st.pop();

    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        while (!st.empty() && a[st.top()] >= a[i]) st.pop();
        R[i] = st.empty() ? n : st.top();
        st.push(i);
    }

    // Count subarrays where each value is minimum
    map<int, int> freq;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        int cnt = (i - L[i]) * (R[i] - i);
        freq[a[i]] += cnt;
    }

    vector<pair<int, int>> res(freq.begin(), freq.end());
    int sz = res.size();

    // Prefix: processed contribution (denominator → 1 then numerator)
    vector<int> prefGain(sz);
    vector<int> prefCount(sz);

    int sumGain = 0;
    int sumCount = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < sz; i++) {
        int x = res[i].first;
        int cnt = res[i].second;

        int val = ((-(x - 1) % MOD + MOD) % MOD 
                  + (x - 1) * inv(x) % MOD) % MOD;

        sumGain = (sumGain + val * cnt % MOD) % MOD;
        prefGain[i] = sumGain;

        sumCount += cnt;
        prefCount[i] = sumCount;
    }

    // Suffix: sum of cnt * inv(x)
    vector<int> suffInv(sz);
    int sumInv = 0;

    for (int i = sz - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        int x = res[i].first;
        int cnt = res[i].second;

        sumInv = (sumInv + cnt * inv(x) % MOD) % MOD;
        suffInv[i] = sumInv;
    }

    // Total number of subarrays
    int totalSubarrays = n * (n + 1) / 2;

    // Process queries
    for (int qi = 0; qi < m; qi++) {
        int K = k[qi];

        if (K < res[0].first) {
            // All in k/x case
            int ans = (base + K % MOD * suffInv[0] % MOD) % MOD;
            cout << ans << '\n';
        }
        else if (K > res.back().first) {
            // All fully reduced + numerator
            int ans = (base 
                      + totalSubarrays % MOD * (K % MOD) % MOD 
                      + prefGain.back()) % MOD;
            cout << ans << '\n';
        }
        else {
            // Binary search split
            int l = 0, h = sz - 1, idx = 0;
            while (l <= h) {
                int mid = (l + h) / 2;
                if (res[mid].first <= K) {
                    idx = mid;
                    l = mid + 1;
                } else h = mid - 1;
            }

            int leftPart = (prefCount[idx] % MOD) * (K % MOD) % MOD;
            leftPart = (leftPart + prefGain[idx]) % MOD;

            int rightPart = 0;
            if (idx < sz - 1) {
                rightPart = (K % MOD) * suffInv[idx + 1] % MOD;
            }

            int ans = (base + leftPart + rightPart) % MOD;
            cout << ans << '\n';
        }
    }

    return 0;
}

    Code
    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define int long long
const int MOD = 998244353;

int power(int a, int b) {
    int res = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) res = (res * a) % MOD;
        a = (a * a) % MOD;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}

int inv(int x) {
    return power(x, MOD - 2);
}

int32_t main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);

    int T = 1; //cin >> T;
    while (T--) {
        int n, m;
        cin >> n >> m;

        vector<int> a(n), k(m);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i];
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) cin >> k[i];

        int c = 0LL, ans = 0LL;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            c = (c + (i + 1) * (inv(a[i]) % MOD)) % MOD;
            ans = (ans + c) % MOD;
        }

        vector<int> L(n), R(n);
        stack<int> st;

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            while (!st.empty() && a[st.top()] > a[i]) st.pop();
            L[i] = st.empty() ? -1 : st.top();
            st.push(i);
        }

        while (!st.empty()) st.pop();

        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            while (!st.empty() && a[st.top()] >= a[i]) st.pop();
            R[i] = st.empty() ? n : st.top();
            st.push(i);
        }

        vector<pair<int,int>> res;
        map<int,int> mp;

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            int cnt = (i - L[i]) * (R[i] - i);
            mp[a[i]] += cnt;
        }

        for (auto &p : mp) {
            res.push_back(p);
        }

        //up till heres just finding segments with min = whatever
        
        vector<int> pd(res.size()); //prefix gain (decr den to make frac = 1 then increase numerator)
        c = 0;

        vector<int> psac(res.size()); //number of subarrays with min<=x
        int pc = 0;

        for (int i = 0; i < (int)pd.size(); i++) {
            c = (c + ((-(res[i].first - 1) + ((res[i].first - 1) * inv(res[i].first) % MOD) + MOD) % MOD) * res[i].second) % MOD;
            pd[i] = c;

            pc += res[i].second;
            psac[i] += pc;
        }

        vector<int> sf(res.size()); //(number of subarrays with min = x)*x, suffix
        c = 0;

        for (int i = (int)sf.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            c = (c + inv(res[i].first) * (res[i].second) % MOD) % MOD;
            sf[i] = c;
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            if (k[i] < res[0].first) {
                cout << (ans + k[i] * sf[0]) % MOD << '\n';
            }
            else if (k[i] > res.back().first) {
                cout << (ans + (n * (n + 1) / 2 % MOD) * k[i] % MOD + pd.back()) % MOD << '\n';
            }
            else {
                int l = 0, h = (int)res.size() - 1;
                int idx = 0;

                while (l <= h) {
                    int u = (l + h) / 2;
                    if (res[u].first <= k[i]) {
                        idx = u;
                        l = u + 1;
                    } else h = u - 1;
                }

                int f = (
                    ans 
                    + (psac[idx] * k[i] % MOD + pd[idx]) % MOD 
                    + ((idx < (int)res.size() - 1) ? (k[i] * sf[idx + 1] % MOD) : 0)
                ) % MOD;

                cout << f << '\n';
            }
        }
    }

    return 0;
}

    0

    cnt can be as large as O(n2)O(n2) (from (i−L[i])∗(R[i]−i)(i−L[i])∗(R[i]−i))

    So, val * cnt can overflow before the % MOD. Using cnt%MODcnt%MOD before multiplying keeps the product within range and preserves the result, since (val * cnt) % MOD == (val * (cnt % MOD)) % MOD.

    Your updated submission now passes TC20, but fails on TC25 (due to the same bug, but in a different place). Hope you can fix it.

Serval

← Rev. 2 →  
+31

Share a more natural solution of G with an (m+1)×(m+1)(m+1)×(m+1) transition matrix.

First, the constraint that (1,1)(1,1) should be covered by segments is equivalent to that of (n,m)(n,m) by symmetry. Thus, let dpi,l,rdpi,l,r denote the number of ways to choose segments for the first ii rows where the segment for the ii-th row is exactly [l,r][l,r], without considering the constraint of (1,1)(1,1). Easy to write out the transition:

dpi,l,r=1+∑[a,b]∩[l,r]≠∅dpi−1,a,bdpi,l,r=1+∑[a,b]∩[l,r]≠∅dpi−1,a,b

Note that [a,b]∩[l,r]=∅[a,b]∩[l,r]=∅ iff either b<lb<l or r<ar<a. Let the prefix sum and the suffix sum as

prei,k=∑r≤kdpi,l,r,sufi,k=∑l≥kdpi,l,rprei,k=∑r≤kdpi,l,r,sufi,k=∑l≥kdpi,l,r

Then

dpi,l,r=1+prei−1,m−prei−1,l−1−sufi−1,r+1dpi,l,r=1+prei−1,m−prei−1,l−1−sufi−1,r+1

Summing up dpdp by the definitions of prepre and sufsuf above gives the transitions between prei,sufiprei,sufi and prei−1,sufi−1prei−1,sufi−1. Flipping the grid horizontally gives the bijection between ways to choose segments of prei,kprei,k and that of sufi,m−k+1sufi,m−k+1, showing that they are equal. Thus, only m+1m+1 states (prepre and a constant 11) should be maintained. The final answer is pren,m−pren,m−1pren,m−pren,m−1.

I think G can be solved for M≤800M≤800 . Not recommended. (It seems like the solution above doesn't work for small modulos or non-prime modulos, so if only matrix multiplication is allowed, then M≤500M≤500 is doable.)

Also, what is the exact reason for the fact that BM doesn't work? What's the time complexity of BM?

Edit: the fastest solutions uses the Reed-Sloane algorithm (BM's extension to non-prime modulos). I guess the time complexity is O(n3)O(n3) or simmilar.

AksLolCoding
9 days ago, hide # |
 
+11
Optimizations to E solution

Read the editorial solution first. Let SS denote the sum of the digits of ss. We can see that the suffix has sum of digits at most 7171, so the sum of digits of the original number must lie in [S−71,S][S−71,S], so we only need to check 7272 possibilities.

Submission: 367238863

In practice, this limit is much smaller (it is actually just 44!), so we can precompute SS over all possible sum of digits of xx for a faster query time, although this is much slower due to small tt (and my horrible constant optimization).

Submission: 367236557

    actualtsg
    9 days ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    I might argue that max suffix sum of digit is at most 70, not 71 because sum of digit = 999999 is not reachable from given problem constraint, so sum of digit 888889 is the worst case here with sum of suffix digit equal to 70: (8+8+8+8+8+9) + (4+9) + (1+3) + (4) = 70

    My submisson checking only 71 possiblities without any precompute: 367072645 (62 ms)

    And yes, in practice precompute is a bit slower than brute force in this case, I also has another submission that precompute all possible sum of digit too, and the result it's a bit slower (it uses >110 MB of memory): 367056743 (93 ms)

    Maybe because my language is pure C (without plus-plus) so my runtime is faster than yours even with the same algorithm x)

Hi! I've been trying to submit F on python lately and many times I faced TLE on test 25. I've tried to optimise it in different ways but still got TLE. From what I've gathered the only things that could possibly slow down my solution are sort and exponentiation modulo, but I'm more biased towards the second option. I'm a bit curious as why these things could possibly affect complexity so much?

For D, I think the problem should be phrased as “Call a vertex v beautiful if all not-necessarily-simple paths in original graph that start at v and that still exist after determining directions of each edge, are alternating in the resulting directed graph.” Because a path in original (undirected) graph could be a->b->a when there is only one edge (a,b). Then, no matter how you choose direction of (a,b), the path a->b->a will be missing an edge, and hence the path becomes invalid. But the problem doesn’t say how an invalid-after-direction path should affect the beauty of the vertices involved. I suppose one can figure out the intended meaning by studying examples, but still it confused me at first. Correct me if I’m missing something.

UPD: I understood the problem now and there is nothing wrong with the phrasing.

Hello,

I would like to clarify that I did not copy or share my solution with anyone during the contest.

For problem 2204D, I solved it by modeling the problem as a bipartite graph check. I used a standard BFS/DFS-based coloring approach, where each node is assigned one of two colors and adjacent nodes are checked for conflicts.

Since bipartite checking is a very common and standard technique, I believe it is possible for independently written solutions to look very similar in structure and implementation.

My solution was written independently during the contest, and I did not use any external sources or public code-sharing platforms.

Please let me know if any further clarification is required.

Thank you.

[submission:2204D]
